Q1: I tried to be very disciplined with my study routine, dedicating about 28 hours a week across different subjects. I would typically block out time in the mornings and evenings, writing out schedules and reading through materials systematically. Despite my best efforts to stay organized, I found that the sheer volume of seven online courses made it difficult to give each one the depth it deserved. I kept detailed notes and tried to review them regularly, but honestly, the anxiety about keeping up sometimes disrupted my focus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and how overwhelmed I was feeling at any given time. I tried to maintain regular study times, but when multiple assignments piled up, everything shifted into crisis mode. Some weeks I would be ahead of schedule, and other weeks I was scrambling just to submit things on time. The unpredictability of my other responsibilities also meant I had to be flexible, even when I preferred structure.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I knew an assignment was coming due or when I needed to check for announcements. I also made it a habit to log in at least once a day to see if there were any updates from instructors or discussion posts I needed to respond to. Sometimes the anxiety of not knowing what I might be missing pushed me to check more frequently than necessary. It became almost compulsive at certain points during the semester.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine fairly early in the semester. I would wake up, make tea, and immediately open the platform to see what was happening across all my courses. It started as a way to manage my anxiety about falling behind, but eventually it just became automatic. The habit stuck because I realized that staying informed helped me feel slightly more in control, even when things were overwhelming.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because I learn best through reading and writing. I would download PDFs and articles, highlight key passages, and then write summaries in my own words. The discussion forums were also helpful because they forced me to articulate my understanding and engage with different perspectives. Video lectures were useful too, but I found myself taking extensive notes while watching, which sometimes meant I had to pause and rewind constantly.
Q6: I tended to ignore some of the optional supplementary videos and external links that weren't directly tied to assignments. It wasn't that I didn't value them, but with seven courses demanding my attention, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. Sometimes I would save them with the intention of coming back later, but that rarely happened. The peer interaction features also got less attention from me than they probably deserved because I was so focused on completing my own work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to either an assignment or the exam material. I also valued activities that helped me understand concepts I was struggling with, even if they took longer. If something helped reduce my anxiety about an upcoming deadline or clarified a confusing topic, it was absolutely worth the investment. The sense of accomplishment after completing a challenging task also motivated me to engage more deeply.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, even though I knew they might help me learn better. Required activities had this weight of obligation that made me prioritize them no matter what, while optional ones got pushed to the bottom of an ever-growing list. I often felt guilty about skipping optional materials because I genuinely wanted to learn everything, but the fear of failing the required components was stronger. It created this constant tension between wanting to explore and needing to survive.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I would download readings and work through them on my tablet without an internet connection, especially when I needed to focus without distractions. I also used physical notebooks extensively, writing out concepts by hand to help them stick in my memory. Sometimes I consulted external textbooks or YouTube videos when the course materials didn't explain something clearly enough for me.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course content but the LMS would show almost nothing. For example, I might spend three hours working through a downloaded PDF, annotating it and creating summary notes, but the system only registered that I accessed the file once. I also did a lot of mental rehearsal and self-testing using my handwritten notes, which obviously left no digital trace. My real learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my preferred method for most reading materials. I would access a document once to download it, then study it extensively offline over several days. This approach helped me concentrate better and reduced my screen time, which was important for managing my stress levels. The LMS data would show one brief access, but in reality, I might have spent hours with that same material across multiple study sessions.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became incredibly difficult, usually when multiple deadlines converged across my seven courses. During those times, I felt scared and overwhelmed, and my attendance suffered because I was trying to catch up on assignments instead of attending live sessions. The anxiety would spike, and even though I have strong resilience, there were moments when I just felt paralyzed by the sheer volume of work. My CBT sessions helped me work through some of these periods, but they were still very challenging.
Q13: Workload was probably the biggest factor affecting my LMS usage. When I was exhausted from juggling so many courses, I would sometimes just skim materials instead of engaging deeply with them. Confusion about certain topics also made me hesitate to engage because I felt like I needed more background knowledge first. Competing responsibilities, like family obligations and part-time work, meant that some weeks the LMS got minimal attention even though I was still trying to learn through other means.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I would go through the motions of accessing materials without really absorbing anything. I might watch a video while my mind was racing with anxiety about other deadlines, or scroll through readings without actually processing the information. These moments usually happened when I was mentally exhausted but still felt compelled to do something productive. The engagement was superficial, driven more by guilt than genuine curiosity.
Q15: I definitely postponed exam preparation even though I knew it was crucial. My focus was always on assignments because they had clear deadlines and I could control the outcome through effort. Exam studying felt more uncertain and anxiety-inducing because I wasn't sure if my preparation methods were effective. I kept telling myself I would review everything properly before the exam, but the constant stream of assignments meant that never really happened the way I planned.
Q16: Deadlines completely dictated my LMS usage patterns. I would become extremely active in the days leading up to an assignment deadline, logging in frequently to check requirements, submit drafts, and participate in discussions for participation credit. After a deadline passed, my activity would drop significantly as I shifted focus to the next urgent task. This cycle repeated throughout the semester, creating these spikes and valleys in my engagement data.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on my engagement. Positive feedback motivated me to maintain my approach and sometimes even explore topics more deeply. However, when feedback was critical or when I received lower scores than expected, it triggered anxiety that sometimes made me avoid similar activities temporarily. I tried to use feedback constructively, but my high anxiety levels meant that negative comments could derail my focus for days.
Q18: The course structure significantly influenced how I participated. When modules were clearly organized with explicit expectations, I felt more confident and engaged more consistently. However, when the structure was ambiguous or when different courses had conflicting organizational approaches, I struggled to keep track of everything. Having seven courses meant I was constantly context-switching, and inconsistent structures across courses made this even harder.
Q19: Clear rubrics and well-organized module pages made engagement much easier for me. When I knew exactly what was expected, I could plan my time effectively and reduce my anxiety. On the other hand, courses with scattered materials or vague instructions made engagement harder because I spent more time trying to figure out what I was supposed to do than actually learning. The discussion forums were helpful when they had clear prompts, but overwhelming when participation requirements were unclear.
Q20: I think students might show high LMS activity but submit little work when they're struggling to understand the material or feeling overwhelmed by anxiety. They might be logging in repeatedly, watching videos multiple times, and reading materials over and over, but still not feeling confident enough to submit work. It could also be that they're preparing extensively but running out of time before deadlines, or that they're perfectionists who can't bring themselves to submit work they consider incomplete.
Q21: This somewhat reflects my experience with exam performance versus assignment work. I completed nearly all my assignments with high scores, but my exam performance was poor despite my efforts. Some students might be efficient learners who don't need to spend much time in the LMS because they grasp concepts quickly or study effectively offline. Low LMS activity doesn't necessarily mean low engagement—it might mean the student has found more effective learning methods elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing of submissions, and frequency of interactions with different materials. They're good at capturing when students are active and which resources they click on. However, they completely miss the quality of engagement, the depth of understanding, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether a student was genuinely focused or just going through the motions, and they miss the emotional and psychological dimensions of learning.
Q23: For me, being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking questions, and trying to connect new concepts to what I already know. It's about active mental involvement, not just passive consumption of content. True engagement includes the emotional investment—caring about understanding, feeling frustrated when something doesn't make sense, and experiencing satisfaction when it finally clicks. It's a holistic experience that goes far beyond clicking through pages.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my extensive offline study habits, my handwritten notes, or the hours I spent mentally reviewing concepts while commuting or before sleep. It wouldn't capture my anxiety levels, the CBT sessions I attended to manage my stress, or the resilience I had to summon just to keep going when things felt impossible. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite failing the exam, I genuinely learned and grew throughout this course—my understanding improved even if my final grade didn't reflect that.
